words: SAM SCHARGER: Not yet.
LOLA GAMBLE CLYDE: Oh, it's just like going into a museum. One thing he has on the
wall
is real cute. It's a little team of pigs hitched up to a little wagon
and they're driving it- and a man named Huff is driving it. And Hirff
came from Grangeville. They took it back to the World Fair at Chicago.
They took it down to thejttic Yukon Exhibition in ?909 and won
aprize. And there they are and he's got that picture hanging there on
the wall. And there's a lot of other old-time pictures. And then in
it, are many replicas of old-time things. And he really knows an aw
ful lot of history and stuff. You'll just love Uncle Bill.

speaker: SS
words: Well, let me ask you about social life wanted to know one

speaker: SS
words: thing about the women in those days; I've had the impression that alot

speaker: SS
words: of them were not in the best of health. That there was a lot of sick

speaker: SS
words: ness among the women who were here in the early days, and there was all

speaker: SS
words: over the country in the early 1900's. Do you find that to have been

speaker: SS
words: so? When you think about it?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so too, that it seemed to be one of the great subjects

speaker: LGC
words: of conversation- was the ill health that they all had. And part of it

speaker: LGC
words: was that they didn't have enough outside interests, I think, and then

speaker: LGC
words: every ache and pain was magnified, and as they used to say, "She feels

speaker: LGC
words: every pain," you know, and maybe some of it was that. But in those

speaker: LGC
words: days they had children one right after the other, you know, and

speaker: LGC
words: be ten children in ten years, and that's pretty hard on women's health.

speaker: LGC
words: And then there was lots of hard work required of the early pioneer wo

speaker: LGC
words: men, and I imagine their health wasn't very good. And doctors were

speaker: LGC
words: very inadequate. They had so few of them. And in the very early days

speaker: LGC
words: most of the children were ushered into the world by the help of the mid

speaker: LGC
words: wife and that sort of thing.

speaker: SS
words: So really, the childbearing was really hard on them.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, it was hard on them.

speaker: SS
words: Took it's toll physically.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, it did. It took it's toll physically. That's right.

speaker: SS
words: Is that because they didn't know enough about- at the time- about how

speaker: SS
words: to take care of that with the least trouble?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I suppose so. And many babies were born without much medical

speaker: LGC
words: attention- without much prenatal care- and very little postnatal

speaker: LGC
words: care. And doctors were few and far between.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of an effect, say, would that have on the mothers to have

speaker: SS
words: not had, you know, a doctor's care? What would that do to a would

speaker: SS
words: that just mean they wouldn't get strong- become more weak?

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. And some of them died in childbirth, if there were com

speaker: LGC
words: plications developed. Mothers died in childbirth because they didn't

speaker: LGC
words: have medical assistance at the time.

speaker: SS
words: Was that fairly common up here, for example?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I think as a little child, I remember two of our neighbor wo

speaker: LGC
words: men dying in childbirth. And so that was quite a—

speaker: SS
words: The large sized family, is one, too. I've noticed that it seems like

speaker: SS
words: the early families were very large. Werent they?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right.

speaker: SS
words: But the way my reading indicates that birth control was not unknown

speaker: SS
words: during that period of time, so that it could have been practiced per

speaker: SS
words: haps if families had wanted to. Didn't they have any knowledge of

speaker: SS
words: birth control?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I don't know. A lot of them had the feeling that lots of children was

speaker: LGC
words: lots of security. And a lot of them their religion kept them

speaker: LGC
words: from practicing what they did know. And I just wouldn't be able to

speaker: LGC
words: answer; I don't know.

speaker: LGC
words: Well, when you talk, you know, the security of the children- was that

speaker: LGC
words: the idea that-from what people have said- was that the idea that a

speaker: LGC
words: big family- you'd have alot of people to help on the farm? Was that

speaker: LGC
words: the thinking behind it?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, it could have been, although I've heard some of them say: One

speaker: LGC
words: father can take care of twelve sons; the twelve sons can't take care

speaker: LGC
words: of one father. And I think that was quite true, too. Each of them

speaker: LGC
words: grew up and went on their own, you know merry way. And Idon't know

speaker: LGC
words: that there was so much taking care of the old folks went on as

speaker: LGC
words: could have. But I think religion entered into a lot of their thinking.

speaker: LGC
words: Not necessarily because they were Catholics, but many of the religions

speaker: LGC
words: taught that the Lord giveth and the Lord taketh away, and this is just

speaker: LGC
words: the will of the Lord that I'm given these children.

speaker: SS
words: You mean the Protestant denominations?

speaker: LGC
words: It's true. Many of the Protestant people felt that way that these were

speaker: LGC
words: gifts straight from heaven and they accepted them as that, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Okay, but then what about- we talked some before about women being a

speaker: SS
words: chattel and that kind of stuff.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And being in almost servitude, because of all they had to do for their

speaker: SS
words: husband.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Now, wouldn't it stand to reason that the only way that they could be

speaker: SS
words: free of that would be if they didn't have big families? I mean, to

speaker: SS
words: have a big family was to accept that.

speaker: SS
words: That's right. Well, Ijust don't know what the feeling behind all

speaker: SS
words: that was with the early day women, but lots of them prided themselves:

speaker: SS
words: I can remember one old lady saying that she'd had sixteen children and

speaker: SS
words: she lived to raise eight of 'em!

speaker: SS
words: What did that mean? She lost the other eight?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, she lost the other eight.

speaker: SS
words: In childbirth or when they were babies?

speaker: LGC
words: Small ones, there were different epidemics. Moscow went through the

speaker: LGC
words: black diphtheria scourge here a couple of times. And at one time some

speaker: LGC
words: of the early histories report that half the children in the town of

speaker: LGC
words: Moscow died with black diphtheria.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, really?

speaker: LGC
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: i didn't know that it was that bad.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, it was that bad.

speaker: SS
words: When do you think about when would that have been?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, it would be in the very early- in the early '70's. 1877, maybe and

speaker: SS
words: '78, along through there they had a bad epidemic of black diphtheria.

speaker: SS
words: Well, thinking about this- what women accepted for their lives, it

speaker: SS
words: sounds to me like- Well, what I'm wondering, do you think that the wo

speaker: SS
words: men then questioned whether they should have an alternative to just

speaker: SS
words: being homemakers and raising afamily? Or did that only come later?

speaker: SS
words: That that was questioned.

speaker: LGC
words: Well,-

speaker: SS
words: You know what I mean.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I know what you mean.

speaker: SS
words: Is that the only way they thought to live at the time?

speaker: SS
words: Well, Ican only answe^from my own mother's viewpoint. My mother was

speaker: SS
words: an educated woman and she had taught- she taught in the first girls'

speaker: SS
words: school in Canada in the Angela College of Victoria, B. C. And when

speaker: SS
words: she moved to this pioneer settlement, she wanted my father to let her

speaker: SS
words: go out and teach school, and oh, that was against the principles of

speaker: SS
words: the old country. The man made the living; it was his privilege and

speaker: SS
words: his right and he wouldn't let her go. But, I've often heard her say

speaker: SS
words: that she wished that my father had let her go and teach school at the

speaker: SS
words: same time because they could have used the money so well. And nearly

speaker: SS
words: all of the farm women had their cows and they would milk them and sell

speaker: SS
words: the cream. And they got most of the egg money, and that was considered

speaker: SS
words: their money that they could do as they pleased. But the grain, the

speaker: SS
words: crop had to go in^or paying for land or machinery or for debts, but

speaker: SS
words: the egg and cream money, the women could have that.

speaker: SS
words: But they spent it not on themselves?

speaker: LGC
words: No, they spent it on the children and on food. Most of it

speaker: LGC
words: went for the children and on food for the table.

speaker: LGC
words: So, in other words, she was really exclusively in control of the chil

speaker: LGC
words: dren and the food.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right. That was her calling.

speaker: LGC
words: What about the household furnishings and the furniture and all that,

speaker: LGC
words: was that all her's, too?

speaker: LGC
words: Well,- and once again, Ican hardly answer it. The men did most of

speaker: LGC
words: this buying, as I remember it. The Scandinavian men, especially. But

speaker: LGC
words: some of them took great pride in their houses and buying a piano for

speaker: LGC
words: their daughters. That was the epitome of gracious living, was to be

speaker: LGC
words: able to get apiano and give the girls music lessons, when Iwas alit

speaker: LGC
words: tle child. And there was some of the families that could do that.

speaker: LGC
words: So, say somejthings like sofas and chairs and that sort of thing- would

speaker: LGC
words: that usually be the husband or wife who would make-

speaker: LGC
words: Well most of the purchases, most of the major purchases, your purchases

speaker: LGC
words: like that much money, would be made by the man. The men would decide

speaker: LGC
words: when they would get it and some of them took pride in having well fur

speaker: LGC
words: nished houses, and that sort of thing, you know.

speaker: LGC
words: Well, Iknow these sorts of things are pretty imponderable, they're

speaker: LGC
words: difficult to figure; But I'm asking you some of these things because

speaker: LGC
words: of the reading that I've done now, when we're looking at women's his

speaker: LGC
words: tory, and history of the nineteenth century. These are the kind of

speaker: LGC
words: questions that they're asking. Why was it this way? And how was it?

speaker: LGC
words: So, they're trying to generalize on what- you know- on some of these

speaker: LGC
words: things. But for other women, maybe sometimes your mother. Was your

speaker: LGC
words: mother unusual in her interest in getting out of the home to teach?

speaker: LGC
words: Do you think that it was common and that most women would have if they

speaker: LGC
words: could have?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, yes, I think if they had been fitted; but^so few were educated

speaker: LGC
words: in those days, you know, there were so few trained, I think most of

speaker: LGC
words: them would have tried, because I remember in later years when they

speaker: LGC
words: opened the pea picking factory and Moscow became the pea capital

speaker: LGC
words: of the world and women could go to town and get jobs in the winter

speaker: LGC
words: picking out the rogue peas from the other peas. Oh, lots of the women

speaker: LGC
words: went out and got jobs and would drive to town every day to pick peas.

speaker: LGC
words: And that gave them a feeling of independence and they got money for

speaker: LGC
words: doing it. And many of the married women who's Children were then in

speaker: LGC
words: school would even get a job in town to bring in a little extra money.

speaker: SS
words: They'd come from the rural areas. They'd come from Troy and further?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, oh, yes, they came from Troy and come into Moscow to pick peas.

speaker: SS
words: When did that start? In the '30's or before?

speaker: SS
words: Maybe after 1925, people started picking peas, because Moscow then had

speaker: SS
words: established a good market for green peas.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what about the dealings of women teachers in the schools? In

speaker: SS
words: the early days Ihad heard that it was largely men. Is that true?

speaker: SS
words: Largely men teachers in the rural schools?

speaker: SS
words: Well, there were some, yes, but right around here that was avery well

speaker: SS
words: accepted position for the women- for the girls, they would grow up to

speaker: SS
words: be teachers.

speaker: SS
words: But, now, I'm trying to figure when- In the early_ pioneering, in the

speaker: SS
words: '80's, say, were there women teachers back that early? In large num

speaker: SS
words: bers?

speaker: SS
words: No, I think that most of the early teachers were men teachers.

speaker: SS
words: So your mother would probably have been unusual as ateacher at that

speaker: SS
words: time?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, that's right, because they weren't trained for it. No, that's

speaker: SS
words: right. Most of them were men teachers. And it wasn't until maybe in

speaker: SS
words: the 'Id's that we saw women coming into that. It was a highly respec

speaker: SS
words: ted job for the young girls to grow up to be teachers, or they could

speaker: SS
words: be nurses. That was a highly respected job.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that it started then because that's when the girls were

speaker: SS
words: able to go to Lewiston Normal and the University?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, Ithink so. Yes, because in 1890- in  came the Lewiston Nor

speaker: SS
words: mal. And that was a fine opportunity for the young girls to go on

speaker: SS
words: and get educations.

speaker: SS
words: And they were going there right away to teach?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes. Yes, as soon as the Lewiston Normal started. We had no

speaker: LGC
words: school of education at the University of Idaho when it first opened.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, there wasn't?

speaker: SS
words: No, Must the liberal arts. But girls did go there. But not with

speaker: SS
words: idea of becoming teachers so much. But after Lewiston Normal star

speaker: SS
words: ted, that was to train teachers and they did go down there. And that

speaker: SS
words: was an ambition with many of them was to go on to school; those that

speaker: SS
words: liked school.

speaker: SS
words: Well, then, do you think- would it be fair to say that probably the

speaker: SS
words: large numbers- or the influx of women into rural school teaching star

speaker: SS
words: ted when Lewiston Normal started? Or do you think it was started be

speaker: SS
words: fore?

speaker: LGC
words: I think it started with Lewiston Normal.

speaker: SS
words: Before that they wouldn't have really had a chance.

speaker: LGC
words: They wouldn't have had any place to get it. And for many years, the

speaker: LGC
words: student nurses had to go to Spokane, to the hospitals there to get nur

speaker: LGC
words: se's training. They would go up to Sacred Heart and get training there.

speaker: LGC
words: But with the coming of Lewiston Normal, started turning out our

speaker: LGC
words: own rural teachers.

speaker: SS
words: Were they going to Cheney before Lewiston Normal for teaching? I've

speaker: SS
words: heard of your contemporaries did go to Cheney.

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes, many of them went to Cheney. And many of them from right around

speaker: LGC
words: here went to Cheney. Yes, Cheney was established- Cheney Normal

speaker: LGC
words: was established ahead of Lewiston

speaker: SS
words: Somewhat earlier?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, somewhat earlier.

speaker: SS
words: Then it seems to me that the problem, in a way, was pushed back fur

speaker: SS
words: ther, because once they were teaching in the rural schools then a young

speaker: SS
words: woman could teach until she married. Right?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And that was built into the work.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: How did people feel about that? You told me some of}that you didn't

speaker: SS
words: mind stopping teaching; but do you think that at the time, that after

speaker: SS
words: this freedom of being a teacher, making your own money and making your

speaker: SS
words: own way, that most of the women didn't mind to just give that all up?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I think a lot of them didn't give it up. Alot of them went on

speaker: LGC
words: teaching after they were married until the Depression hit, and then

speaker: LGC
words: married women couldn't get jobs, because they felt that the single women

speaker: LGC
words: needed the jobs or man who was head of a family should have the

speaker: LGC
words: job. So married women weren't allowed to teach, during the Depression

speaker: LGC
words: No one would hire them..

speaker: SS
words: But before that, people- teachers have told me that in the rural coun

speaker: SS
words: try, that not only was it custom that they stop teaching when they mar

speaker: SS
words: ried, but in a lot of school districts they had it in the contract,

speaker: SS
words: that they couldn't be, married

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right. That's true..

speaker: SS
words: That didn't seem like it gave much choice.

speaker: LGC
words: That didn't give them much choice, did it? Well, they had to be real

speaker: LGC
words: ly in love with the man they married, you know, to give up their in

speaker: LGC
words: dependence.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, it encouraged romantic marriage?

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. It encouraged it, you know.

speaker: SS
words: But in terms of- just from the point of view of fairness, it seems that

speaker: SS
words: that meant for most of these people they had a choice between career

speaker: SS
words: and marriage.

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. That's about the way it has been you know, through the

speaker: LGC
words: years.

speaker: SS
words: I'm just wondering if they had that as a rule in the rural schools in

speaker: SS
words: so many of them that you couldn't be married and teach. To give the

speaker: SS
words: the person the option, would seem alot fairer than to say, "You can't

speaker: SS
words: teach, if you're married."

speaker: LGC
words: Well, many of the rural schools did hire married women up until the

speaker: LGC
words: time of the Depression. Yes, many of them did. I knew quite a few

speaker: LGC
words: married women who taught around Moscow in the immediate vicinity. And

speaker: LGC
words: they went on teaching and that sort of thing. But after the Depression

speaker: LGC
words: hit, then most of the schools wouldn't hire a married woman, because

speaker: LGC
words: they felt that they should save the job for some single woman or for

speaker: LGC
words: some head of a family.

speaker: SS
words: Did most of these woman that did teach quit teaching to raise their

speaker: SS
words: family and then come back to teaching. I mean were they older women?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. I think most of them were older women or women with

speaker: LGC
words: out children or women with just one child. They weren't women with a

speaker: LGC
words: lot of children, and a lot of household responsibility. I think that

speaker: LGC
words: was pretty well established.

speaker: SS
words: How old would the youngest child have to be before a woman would feel

speaker: SS
words: comfortable with going back to teaching?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I think it would have to be of school age and then they could

speaker: LGC
words: take the child with them to school or put them in school someplace.

speaker: LGC
words: I think that was the way most of them did, as I remember.

speaker: SS
words: It does to seem to me, for instance with yourself with the choice that

speaker: SS
words: you made to marry; did you find it difficult- somewhat difficult- to

speaker: SS
words: make the adjustment of being totally at home instead of being out in

speaker: LGC
words: Well,]l had avery wonderful, understanding, lenient husband. Very

speaker: LGC
words: indulgent husband. And he just said, "Lola, you just go and do your

speaker: LGC
words: thing and I'll do my thing." And he let me sit on school boards.

speaker: LGC
words: And one time.I was never a bored farmwife, I just sat on all the boards

speaker: LGC
words: in town. And at one time I was on fourteen different boards Latah

speaker: LGC
words: County and the town of Moscow. School boards,.library boards, Red Cross,

speaker: LGC
words: Gritman Hospital, Health, County Health Unit.

speaker: SS
words: Didn't you find it borjf\o sit on all those boards?

speaker: LGC
words: No, I just had fun.

speaker: SS
words: How about your younger years, when you were first married?

speaker: LGC
words: When I was first married, I'll tell you, Iwas just so busy. We had

speaker: LGC
words: a great depression hit and we were down to twenty-eight cents, you know

speaker: LGC
words: and I had about seventeen hired men to cook for and four little child

speaker: LGC
words: ren, one right after the other. Four children all under four years

speaker: LGC
words: of age. I didn't have time for any regrets or any thing else. Iwas

speaker: LGC
words: just too busy trying to keep food on the table for everybody.

speaker: LGC
words: Let's talk a little bit about unmarried women. I'm curious to know

speaker: LGC
words: what you think- It seems from what I've read, and some of what I've

speaker: LGC
words: seen, people who didn't marry. Now, do you feel that- how would you

speaker: LGC
words: explain why a lot of people didn't marry in those days. It seems m

speaker: LGC
words: some ways there was a lot of that back in those days of people deciding

speaker: LGC
words: not to. Was it that the women were too educated for the men who were

speaker: LGC
words: around or something?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, the right man just hadn't come along.K Put it that way. And

speaker: LGC
words: some were unsatisfied with the kind of men they met, you know.

speaker: LGC
words: Of pressure on young women to marry.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so, too. I think the mothers were always afraid the girls

speaker: LGC
words: would be old maids, you know. They'd urge them to get out and meet

speaker: LGC
words: young people and that sort of thing, you know. That that was the ex

speaker: LGC
words: pected thing to do. And all the mothers thought their daughters should

speaker: LGC
words: be the belle of the ball, you know and follow along in the way they had

speaker: LGC
words: done. I think that there was that feeling among them.

speaker: SS
words: But talking about the choice between marriage and career; do you think

speaker: SS
words: that some of the women did make a choice for a job and independence,

speaker: SS
words: freedom-—

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think that's right. I think that there were many of the highly

speaker: LGC
words: intelligent girls didn't marry because they did like their work and

speaker: LGC
words: their jobs and they would have to give up too much. I think. I think

speaker: LGC
words: that's true.

speaker: SS
words: Well, it seemed to me that with the— would you say that there was a

speaker: SS
words: generally high level of education among the girls than the boys?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. Their services weren't needed so badly on the farm.

speaker: LGC
words: The girls. The^oys were needed so badly farming in the rural communi

speaker: LGC
words: ties. And they'd always figure, well, when this boy got out of schoolsometimes

speaker: LGC
words: that was just right out of the eighth grade- why, we'll buy

speaker: LGC
words: another piece of land and start him up farming. And that went on a

speaker: LGC
words: lot and then in the very, very, early times the boys stayed home and

speaker: LGC
words: helped their fathers in the spring and in the fall and maybe in the

speaker: LGC
words: very early days, it would just be a couple of months in the summer

speaker: LGC
words: they had school, and later on, it was just a few months in the winter

speaker: LGC
words: that the older boys were allowed to go to school. And then they couldn't

speaker: LGC
words: be working out in the fields and so on so they'd go to school for a

speaker: LGC
words: little while.

speaker: SS
words: So, the girls because they weren't needed-

speaker: LGC
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: Had the freedom to go

speaker: SS
words: Yes, the girls had more freedom to go to school. Yes, I think that's true.

speaker: SS
words: Now, an educated young woman, could she easily find herself in a po

speaker: SS
words: sition where there wasn't anybody around who was her intellectual- who

speaker: SS
words: could be her companion, that way?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, Ithink so. Ithink that's right. Ithink they educated them

speaker: SS
words: selves out of the market. Ithink sometimes that happened. Ithink

speaker: SS
words: oftenlthat happened.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think men tended to be afraid of brainy women? Or not like

speaker: SS
words: brainy women?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, that's right. Ithink that that's true, yes. Ithink most of

speaker: SS
words: them -"Beware of these schoolmarms, they'll boss you around the rest

speaker: SS
words: of your life!" Although in the early days, the school marms were

speaker: SS
words: geenrally quite the belles of the balls. They set the social pace.

speaker: SS
words: What the new teacher wore and what she did was very much the pattern

speaker: SS
words: for the rest of the girls of the community.

speaker: SS
words: Really? Of the young wives, too?

speaker: LGC
words: No, I lived at my mother's house and came in from the country every

speaker: LGC
words: day.

speaker: SS
words: How far out was that?

speaker: LGC
words: It's about four and a half miles out and I walked most of the time.

speaker: SS
words: Really, you walked into town?

speaker: LGC
words: I walked a lot of the time.

speaker: LGC
words: Daily?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, not daily, sometimes- I did have an old car that we drove part

speaker: LGC
words: of the time, and I caught a ride with some neighbors that went to the

speaker: LGC
words: convent, sometimes. But lots and lots of times I walked because I'd

speaker: LGC
words: be too late to catch a ride going home. I'd have to stay for play

speaker: LGC
words: practice; I had to stay for to go on debate teams- places and for prac

speaker: LGC
words: tice.

speaker: SS
words: Was this for one year, that you did this?

speaker: LGC
words: I did it one year. I went one year to Lewiston Normal; one year to

speaker: LGC
words: the University of Idaho and the rest I did by correspondence and sum

speaker: LGC
words: mer school.

speaker: SS
words: Really. There's a couple of things about- I've heard a lot- I've

speaker: SS
words: heard the men talk about the fraternities at the time and a lot of the

speaker: SS
words: country boys didn't go into fraternities; too much money..

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah, that's right; too much money.

speaker: SS
words: And they have mixed feelings about the fraternities. They were rather

speaker: SS
words: exclusive.

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Were the sororities the same way?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes, in those days they were very exclusive and they were very

speaker: LGC
words: snooty and they - yes, there was lots of heartache and heartbreak

speaker: LGC
words: over them. There wasn't on my account, because, like I say, I was

speaker: LGC
words: twenty-six years old. I'd been a schoolteacher and I was ambitious.

speaker: LGC
words: I wanted to make good grades and with all the hours I took and all the

speaker: LGC
words: papers I had to grade, there was no room left for anything else.

speaker: SS
words: You were there for business?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. I was mature. And sometimes the girls at Forney Hall would

speaker: LGC
words: invite me to come and stay all night with them if it got too late and

speaker: LGC
words: they knew I had so far to go home, they would invite me to come and

speaker: LGC
words: stay and I did. And some of those girls are still my lifelong friends.

speaker: LGC
words: Those girls that would have me come there and stay and I did that. I

speaker: LGC
words: had no time for it. I went into a couple of honoraries; education honorary, debate

speaker: LGC
words: honorary and so on. And the thing I had to ask wasif

speaker: LGC
words: I had to memorize a lot of stuff , I don't want in, because I

speaker: LGC
words: haven't the time to go memorize it. And they took me without memo

speaker: LGC
words: rizing all the rigamarole and stuff.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know of cases where girl's hearts were broken?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes. The silly, mothers back home had pounded into their

speaker: LGC
words: heads they had to make sororitx- One girl tried to kill herself,

speaker: LGC
words: year she hadn't made a sorority, and it was just ridiculous,

speaker: LGC
words: you know. It was rididulous. And some of them- they would

speaker: LGC
words: divide them up according to chuch are you going to? And if you are

speaker: LGC
words: going to a certain church then you join this sorority. Gee, the

speaker: LGC
words: church members would just simply be so mad at you. And there was just

speaker: LGC
words: too much emphasis on it.

speaker: SS
words: Did that mean that the sorority and fraternity life could easily make

speaker: SS
words: it more difficult for the kid to study, and just take him away from

speaker: SS
words: his study?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. You see the big long lists of chapter names they had

speaker: LGC
words: to memorize, and the national officers and then all the pledge of

speaker: LGC
words: allegiance, and symbolism. Get all that stuff memorized, you know, in

speaker: LGC
words: order to be initiated. I don't know how they got their lessons. If

speaker: LGC
words: they were getting their history they'd have been better off. I just

speaker: LGC
words: thought it was a waste of time, myself. But when my girls got that

speaker: LGC
words: age, they all joined and I let them. I said, "You just go and find out

speaker: LGC
words: for yourself, you know, if that's what you want." But they had it a

speaker: LGC
words: little easier because they had more money and things to go on. And

speaker: LGC
words: they were all real good students, and most of them could use good students.

speaker: LGC
words: And that was oneAhing about the sororities, they made them

speaker: LGC
words: keep their grades up and that was good, because they put them down at

speaker: LGC
words: the study table and the older sisters helped the younger ones and

speaker: LGC
words: all that was good in that respect and it did- I think it had a good

speaker: LGC
words: effect on their morals, because if they hadn't been pretty fine little

speaker: LGC
words: gals, none of the sororities would accept them.

speaker: SS
words: That's what I wanted to ask you. Those days, in the '20's, do you

speaker: SS
words: think that relations before marriage were really very uncommon, or-

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, sure they did. I think there were lots of babies born- lots of

speaker: LGC
words: girls had to quit school and hurry and go home. It happened all along.

speaker: LGC
words: And there were mothers covering up, they just weren't so open about it.

speaker: LGC
words: But then it was such a disgrace and such a stigma and so on and so forth,

speaker: LGC
words: The poor little girls would be about to kill themselves over it, and I

speaker: LGC
words: think there was just as Much going on, only it was all kept so in the

speaker: LGC
words: dark. And it was so much more disgraceful and terrible, you know.

speaker: LGC
words: Nowdays the gals have sense enough to say they did it on purpose and

speaker: LGC
words: they want to raise their babies. I think it's smart.

speaker: SS
words: What about Dean French? Do you think she probably knew it was going

speaker: SS
words: on and-

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes, she was just sticking her head in the sand, she wanted to believe

speaker: LGC
words: it didn't go on and that her little girls wouldn't think about

speaker: LGC
words: it. Oh, she must have known if she had a lick of sense and all.

speaker: SS
words: It was a lot more difficult in those days compared to how, when it's

speaker: SS
words: sanctioned. They don't have to hide it.

speaker: LGC
words: They don't have to worry. No, no, they don't have to worry, and I

speaker: LGC
words: think it's better. It's better for the,Jmental health, better for their

speaker: LGC
words: physical health; it's better all the way around. I think they've come

speaker: LGC
words: a long way, myself.

speaker: SS
words: Was birthcontrol information available in those days? In the '20's?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, no.

speaker: SS
words: Could you get it in college?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, no, no.

speaker: SS
words: Planned parenthood?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, no, no. No! Heavens, I had four babies in four years and I was

speaker: LGC
words: one of the smart girls! You know what I mean. And hush, hush, and

speaker: LGC
words: doctors wouldn't even tell you. No. After I had four babies, why

speaker: LGC
words: they wouldn't even tell me then. No. No. There was great hush and

speaker: LGC
words: secrets.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't realize it was so bad, but it doesn't surprise me.

speaker: LGC
words: No. That's the way it was. No. You didn't talk about such things

speaker: LGC
words: even. Uh-huh. You didn't talk about such things! I had a boys from

speaker: LGC
words: India here as a foreign exchange student and so he told me, he said,

speaker: LGC
words: "I'm going back to India and I'm going to make two blades of rice grow

speaker: LGC
words: where only one grew before. I'm going to cut India's birth population

speaker: LGC
words: in half, I'm going to cut it in half." He said, "Now, Mama, I want you

speaker: LGC
words: to tell me all about planned parenthood." I said, "I'll tell you,

speaker: LGC
words: honey, the way it is: you start in planning after you know there's going

speaker: LGC
words: to be another baby! The babies don't know a darn thing about this

speaker: LGC
words: planned parenthood. And the best thing you can do is go up there on

speaker: LGC
words: the campus and ask those fraternity boys, I think they can clue you in."

speaker: LGC
words: So I saw him again just a year ago. He said, "I've done pretty well,

speaker: LGC
words: I have eleven sisters and they're all doctors. And in India the women

speaker: LGC
words: doctors serve the women and after they've had two children my sisters

speaker: LGC
words: tell them, "That's enough." And give them the means, give them the Pill."

speaker: LGC
words: And I said, "I think that's just fine." And he said, "I sell Shell fer

speaker: LGC
words: tilizer and fly my own plane," And he was from Madras, "And I am ma

speaker: LGC
words: king three blades of rice grow for inhere one grew before. And we're

speaker: LGC
words: doing alright." (Laughter) So, you see

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the mores in college, now, at the University were

speaker: SS
words: different than they were in the country, say in the rural areas around?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. Yes, I think that the girls out in the country- they

speaker: LGC
words: were more intent on just getting a man- and dragging sane man in oUd

speaker: LGC
words: marry him and then they were all settled right there. They weren't

speaker: LGC
words: going on to college. They weren't going out for careers. They wanted

speaker: LGC
words: to become a farmer's wife, most of them, and most of them just did that.

speaker: SS
words: Did that mean that they were much less likely to be fooling around,

speaker: SS
words: say before marriage? In the country?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, no, I think it went on too, every, But most of the gals seemed

speaker: LGC
words: to think that was one way of catching a nice young farmer was just to

speaker: LGC
words: get pregnant and by golly, we'll just have a shotgun wedding, and

speaker: LGC
words: there'd we'd be. They had her made.

speaker: SS
words: They were common, I know that.

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes. I think it was just as common. I think it was just as com

speaker: LGC
words: mon then as it is now. I really do. I don't think they need to de

speaker: LGC
words: cry the young people. I think they just know better now. They are

speaker: LGC
words: freer, they are more open, they're smarter now.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was a difference in attitude- I'm thinking now about

speaker: SS
words: the people who were high status and stuff; was there a difference

speaker: SS
words: between those who had high status, like the Mc Connels, who had de

speaker: SS
words: finitely achieved high status, and those who were kind of the social

speaker: SS
words: climbers? Who were upwardly mobile.

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was a difference in them- I mean, in their attitude?

speaker: SS
words: I have been told by some people, that people who were genuinely high

speaker: SS
words: status didn't try to show it. They weren't ostentatious, but other

speaker: SS
words: people who were up and coming were always trying to put the other people down.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think that's true in any society. We had real ladies and

speaker: LGC
words: gentlemen in town; just real ladies, fine people, fine, good , Christian

speaker: LGC
words: women and! Clarice Sampson's mother was one of them. Mrs. Moody

speaker: LGC
words: was one of the kindest, sweetest, Christian women ever lived. And

speaker: LGC
words: they were always helpful and kind, and doing the right thing because

speaker: LGC
words: it was right. And trying to be good Christians and good neighbors and

speaker: LGC
words: they weren't too concerned about the little fashionable tricks and

speaker: LGC
words: traits. There were a lot of good, fine, bright, brilliant women. And

speaker: LGC
words: with the coming of the University they really brought in a new dimen

speaker: LGC
words: sion. Lots of those fine early-day professor's wives were smart and

speaker: LGC
words: kind and gracious and highly intelligent; well-bred. And they brought

speaker: LGC
words: a new dimension into the community.

speaker: SS
words: Were they very involved in the community, or were they—?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, for a while there was a great division about this town and gown

speaker: LGC
words: stuff, you know. The town women would have a society one time- and

speaker: LGC
words: then the faculty women. And then one year we were going to have a

speaker: LGC
words: faculty wife for president and the next year the town women get to

speaker: LGC
words: have it. You know, they went on- well, exaggerating the difference

speaker: LGC
words: between town and gown. But when I was a young farm wife and my child

speaker: LGC
words: ren were growing up, I found that I had far more in common with the

speaker: LGC
words: faculty wives than I did with the town wives. We were more interested

speaker: LGC
words: in the same things. We were interested in bettering the conditions

speaker: LGC
words: for everybody's children. That's why I worked hard on the health unit.

speaker: LGC
words: had no health unit here. And when I had taught in the Normal School

speaker: LGC
words: in South Idaho, we just routinely vaccinated every child. Gave them all

speaker: LGC
words: their shots right at the school. And when my children started to school

speaker: LGC
words: here they had none of that. So I started questioning too and many of

speaker: LGC
words: the faculty wives had come from places where sure, just routinely

speaker: LGC
words: went through the schools and vaccinated them. We had dentists come

speaker: LGC
words: out and fill the childrens' teeth right there at the school for fif

speaker: LGC
words: ty cents a child. And if anybody couldn't pay for it, the school paid

speaker: LGC
words: for it. And we bought all the books in the normal school where I

speaker: LGC
words: taught. And there were a lot of things. And I started a PTA here in

speaker: LGC
words: Moscow and was the first president. Because, I thought, "My goodness,

speaker: LGC
words: that's one thing I can use my education and my training and the things

speaker: LGC
words: I'd seen done out in other places."

speaker: SS
words: When was that, when the PTA started?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, they'd had a little Mothers' Club before that. I think it must

speaker: LGC
words: have been about '35, probably. And about that time we put in the Heal,

speaker: LGC
words: Ed Snowman, my next door neighbor down here was county com

speaker: LGC
words: missioner, and so the first thing we did was put me on for the Health

speaker: LGC
words: Unit; to head a committee.

speaker: SS
words: What you're saying then is, it seems to me, that the faculty women in

speaker: SS
words: tended to be more interested in those things—

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. More interested. And they were the people I could count

speaker: LGC
words: on. As head of PTA I'd get the faculty- they would vote 100 percent

speaker: LGC
words: for these things. And the town women took less interest. Lot of them,

speaker: LGC
words: of course, were picking peas and gone all day and couldn't come to PTA.

speaker: SS
words: But even the middle class-

speaker: LGC
words: Even the middle class they attended. "Well, my husband- well, I can't

speaker: LGC
words: take a stand on that, it'd hurt my husband's business." YOu know.

speaker: LGC
words: The faculty were the ones that really saw the need for these things.

speaker: SS
words: So there was a certain amount of controversy over having the Health

speaker: SS
words: Unit and that kind of thing?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, of course, there was. Yes, there was a controversy.

speaker: SS
words: Is that because-

speaker: LGC
words: It was socialized medicine and^were sticking out our necks, and yet

speaker: LGC
words: we were just getting over the Depression- just coming out from underwhen

speaker: LGC
words: nobody had had doctors. And so we were all just struggling along.

speaker: LGC
words: But this is something we had done back in the school and it was a won

speaker: LGC
words: derful thing. And I worked hard on that: getting the PTA going, because

speaker: LGC
words: there were many things we could do. And we ran an exchange there

speaker: LGC
words: at the school, we'd bring in all the outgrown overshoes, for instance,

speaker: LGC
words: and put them in big boxes and some other mother could take those over

speaker: LGC
words: shoes our kids had outgrown. We didn't sell 'em, we j ust gave them.

speaker: LGC
words: We had a room set up. We had a room there of patterns little dres

speaker: LGC
words: ses for girls. We got the little patterns and when they got too small

speaker: LGC
words: we took 'em in there and the next mother could check 'em out. And remnanents

speaker: LGC
words: of clothing. We'd bring them in. We had great button boxes.

speaker: LGC
words: The library now has a button box where people go and get buttons. Well,

speaker: LGC
words: we had it at PTA. They'd go in and pick out buttons. Warm winter coats

speaker: LGC
words: and caps. Instead of giving them to a rummage sale we hauled them in

speaker: LGC
words: the school and PTA had women volunteers and you'd go in and see if

speaker: LGC
words: some of these coats and caps would fit your kids. And it made

speaker: LGC
words: it more democratic because everybody went; we all went. And it wasn't

speaker: LGC
words: that we'd gone to a rummage sale and bought 'em for next to nothing, we

speaker: LGC
words: went in there and we took somebody else's clothes for our kids and gave

speaker: LGC
words: ours to somebody else smaller. And those were all good,progressive

speaker: LGC
words: things.

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: LGC
words: Uh-huh. Good, progressive things. I put in the hot lunch program, too.

speaker: LGC
words: The government was just starting in to sponsor it. And there was a

speaker: LGC
words: big argument the school board that, "Heavens, wasn't that something. What more would

speaker: LGC
words: the district have to do?" And so, I served

speaker: LGC
words: on that committee to put in the first hot lunch and helped cook the

speaker: LGC
words: first lunch.

speaker: SS
words: When do you think that was?

speaker: LGC
words: That was about '35, too. '35 and '36, along in there. Maybe '34.

speaker: SS
words: Were there some people that didn't see the use in the PTA at the time?

speaker: SS
words: When it started?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, . We were a bunch of upstarts, and they didn't want any

speaker: LGC
words: PTAs around there. Yeah, that was nogoodl

speaker: SS
words: Why would they not want the PTA?

speaker: LGC
words: We would be- the next thing we'd be wanting more new schools. And that

speaker: LGC
words: was right! We went out and carried the ball for new schools. Of course

speaker: LGC
words: we did. We wanted a new high school and we got it! And all those were

speaker: LGC
words: good things. And all that came about through PTA work. And it

speaker: LGC
words: was good work. And we had fun at the same time. We did a lot of good

speaker: LGC
words: things. And the thing that made it so important was, it was the peak of

speaker: LGC
words: the Depression. People were still— and we put in the lunch and milk.

speaker: LGC
words: Everybody had to have milk. Give them the half pint of milk, right

speaker: LGC
words: there, and anybody couldn't afford it, why let the government subsidize

speaker: LGC
words: it! And, of course, oh, my, that was just terrible. But we did it in

speaker: LGC
words: spite of them anyway. And now they ^buldn't get along without the hot

speaker: LGC
words: lunch, you know. But there was lots of objecting ye$. I remember the

speaker: LGC
words: superintendent of schools saying to me, "Well, Lola, the first thing

speaker: LGC
words: that you'll be wanting, you'll be wanting us to subsidize mothers to

speaker: LGC
words: have babies!" And so, I said to that superintendent, "I could just

speaker: LGC
words: use a little subsidy myself I I have four bright ones for you, and I

speaker: LGC
words: feel the government should subsidize me; I've got four of 'em for you.

speaker: LGC
words: And they're all going to be good, smart children and get along in schooll"

speaker: LGC
words: Wasn't that nasty? He said to me, "You'll be wanting to subsidize these

speaker: LGC
words: parents for having children." I said, "I could use a little subsidy

speaker: LGC
words: right now!"

speaker: SS
words: So that's another example of really mostly women doing the work, isn't

speaker: SS
words: it?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, yeah. There weren't so many women work ing then, the married women

speaker: LGC
words: weren't teaching. And we did all that PTA business and that was

speaker: LGC
words: good. And we would volunteer our time and go up there and help 'em

speaker: LGC
words: pick out the coats and the overshoes that were outgrown we kept

speaker: LGC
words: track of it. We checked out the patterns.

speaker: SS
words: Now, the distinction between the university women and the local women

speaker: SS
words: we were talking about; that was still existing in the *30*s?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, vt was still there, because even in PTA they wanted to have a fac

speaker: LGC
words: ulty- they wanted to write this into the constitution, when we wrote

speaker: LGC
words: it up: One year we'll have a faculty wife and the next year a town wife

speaker: LGC
words: and they did that a couple of times. Finally, I said, "That^s just ri

speaker: LGC
words: diculous. What we are talking about is mothers, we're not talking about

speaker: LGC
words: We're talking about mothers of children. And what dif

speaker: LGC
words: ference does it make which mother? Where they are? A mother of a child

speaker: LGC
words: is going to be president." So we got that done away with. And then,

speaker: LGC
words: of course, after the war the Red Cross work and everything came along,

speaker: LGC
words: we quit a lot of those activities, because then we became accep

speaker: LGC
words: ted. It was accepted that they'd have hot lunches. It was accepted.

speaker: LGC
words: And then a little later after that they consolidated and we had consolidation

speaker: LGC
words: in the schools, and that was another ball of wax. And then

speaker: LGC
words: I went on the school board, and I helped put through the books for the

speaker: LGC
words: children. That we would furnish books and rent them to the children.

speaker: LGC
words: We wouldn't give them to them for nothing; we would rent the school

speaker: LGC
words: books to the children. And that was good.

speaker: SS
words: But you would say, that you counted more on the faculty wives as being

speaker: SS
words: progressive?

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. And really the people you could count on to help you in

speaker: LGC
words: anything that was good and forwarding looking for the community, you'd

speaker: LGC
words: get the faculty wives to help you, and they would.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much— do you think there was much active antipathy between

speaker: SS
words: faculty wives and town wives?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, yes, in the early years. And even yet there is some feeling, you

speaker: LGC
words: know. "Well, we're not educated. These faculty women, they-" You

speaker: LGC
words: know, I think there's still a little even, you know, I

speaker: LGC
words: tell them I belong to the League of Women Voters, oh, my, some of the

speaker: LGC
words: old-time women think that almost as bad as being a communist!

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: LGC
words: And I told them that I belong to Common Cause. The other day someone

speaker: LGC
words: was asking me about who I was going to vote for, and I said, "Well,I

speaker: LGC
words: don't know, because I haven't studied it. I belong to Common Cause."

speaker: LGC
words: Well, these dear, little women at the church said, "Isn't that some

speaker: LGC
words: kind of a communist front?"

speaker: SS
words: Well, was the League going in the '30's?

speaker: LGC
words: No. No, we didn't get it going until the '40's. We didn't get it going

speaker: LGC
words: til the '50's, into the first of the '50Ts. The League was going about

speaker: LGC
words: 49 when I was on the school board, because I joined it then. We ,wanted

speaker: LGC
words: to get tiflfough a bond issue and those women got out there and just

speaker: LGC
words: worked like dogs getting that bond issue through. It was League of

speaker: LGC
words: Women Voters that put it through.

speaker: SS
words: For a school?

speaker: LGC
words: Uh-huh. The school. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Was the League a very controversial group here, to start with?

speaker: LGC
words: Was there much opposition to it? It certainly seems like uppity wo

speaker: LGC
words: men, you know, getting together and making up their own minds about

speaker: LGC
words: these things.

speaker: LGC
words: Well, no, I always thought it was great stuff.

speaker: SS
words: I certainly agree with you.

speaker: LGC
words: I thought it was great stuff. A lot of the women though had been

speaker: SS
words: Black Man?

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah, we played Black Man.

speaker: SS
words: What was that?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, they'd run out to catch you, you know, and you'd run from one base

speaker: LGC
words: to another. And then the man out in the middle he was supposed to be

speaker: LGC
words: a black man. But the boys, they'd laugh just as hard as anybody about

speaker: LGC
words: playing Black Man and they'd be the Black Man, too, you know. And then

speaker: LGC
words: they'd

speaker: LGC
words: sometimes take a hold of our hands and^run 'em through and they'd

speaker: LGC
words: help the little ones get through and not get caught.

speaker: SS
words: You mean the older boys?

speaker: LGC
words: The Settle boys.

speaker: SS
words: The older boys lied to play with the girls.

speaker: LGC
words: Uh-huh. And we all played together. And I remember us girls crying

speaker: LGC
words: when the Settle boys quite school because they wouldn't be there to

speaker: LGC
words: play Black Man with us anymore.

speaker: SS
words: They quit to ?

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah, they moved. They moved to to town. They moved off the farm

speaker: LGC
words: they'd been farming. Moved into Moscow. And boy, we just felt awful

speaker: LGC
words: bad that the Settles boys had left us.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think they were fully accepted out there? Among the people?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. I think out there on the farm- I remember many people- I remember

speaker: LGC
words: one little- later on, who was my special friend

speaker: LGC
words: too. you know. He was on the school board down in another school dis

speaker: LGC
words: trict and there were a couple of other gentlemen on the board and this

speaker: LGC
words: little girl went out there to teach said, "Well, I have three men on

speaker: LGC
words: my school board, and the only white man among them is Gene Settles."

speaker: LGC
words: And he was very fair and very fine with everybody. Of course, my folks

speaker: LGC
words: had no feeling of discrimination. Gene worked for us when I was a

speaker: LGC
words: little girl and every evening he was planning for the state examinations

speaker: LGC
words: to pass the eighth grade examination to high school, so my mother

speaker: LGC
words: would set him down every evening and help him learn all the state and

speaker: LGC
words: county officials and help him with his mathmatics and help him with the

speaker: LGC
words: things so he could pass. And he did, he passed. And, oh, my mother

speaker: LGC
words: was so happy because Gene had passed the examination; could go on to

speaker: LGC
words: high school.

speaker: SS
words: Would they go to the dances?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, as a little girl I never went to the dances, so I really don't

speaker: LGC
words: know. I really don't know. But they were certainly awfully well ac

speaker: LGC
words: cepted out at that little rural school. I was in classes the two

speaker: LGC
words: younger boys; with Booker T. Washington Settles and who later becsme

speaker: LGC
words: a teacher at Carver College, and married a woman who was a profes

speaker: LGC
words: sor there. They were both professors and came out here to Idaho to

speaker: LGC
words: summer school a couple of summers. And she was a gracious lady, too,

speaker: LGC
words: in her own right. And I think Moscow has always been very, very

speaker: LGC
words: friendly toward especially the Settles family. They were just high

speaker: LGC
words: class farm people.

speaker: SS
words: There were some hard things about living.

speaker: LGC
words: There are sorrowful bad things and we're not so sorry.

speaker: SS
words: About leaving.

speaker: LGC
words: About leaving. If it were all the birds and the flowers on Paradise

speaker: LGC
words: Hill, we'd want to stay forever. Now that's right.

speaker: SS
words: You know when I was asking you about the size of families.

speaker: LGC
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: didn't you tend to have smaller families than your parents had?

speaker: LGC

speaker: LGC
words: from a fine old druggist down in Genesee and when I was pregnant with

words: the fourth child he stopped me on the street and he said, " ., I think

speaker: LGC
words: you've had enough babies." And I said, "Ive been thinking so myself."

speaker: LGC
words: He said, "You come in here and I will show you what to use." And he

speaker: LGC
words: got me one of those little rubber contraceptives deals. And the jelly

speaker: LGC
words: that went with it.

speaker: SS
words: A diaphragm.

speaker: LGC
words: A diaphragm is what it was; and I used it. And there were four and

speaker: LGC
words: that was it. And four was enough.

speaker: SS
words: common to many of the women who were your contemporaries?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, sure. Many of them,1 they didn't know what to do. It was such a

speaker: LGC
words: secret and the doctors wouldn't tell you and there was no planned pa

speaker: LGC
words: renthood. There was no nothing.

speaker: SS
words: But still that change. I mean, it must have been hard to find out or

speaker: SS
words: they wouldn't have-

speaker: LGC
words: We needed to find out. And I even went to these health units that I'd

speaker: LGC
words: helped organize and start, and well, they were very reticent. They

speaker: LGC
words: couldn't tell you.

speaker: SS
words: They were afraid?

speaker: LGC
words: They were afraid, I think. And my doctor, the doctor who delivered

speaker: LGC
words: these children one right after the other, he said only to me, "Oh,

speaker: LGC
words: Mother, you're just the kind of a girl who should have lots of babies.

speaker: LGC
words: You can have them easily. You've had no trouble. And look at the fine,

speaker: LGC
words: bright, husky children you have. You should have a lot of 'em!" That's

speaker: LGC
words: what my doctor said to me. And so I said to the doctor, "Yes, we al

speaker: LGC
words: ways are able to pay our doctor bills." Said I to him! Yeah, isn't

speaker: LGC
words: that something?

speaker: SS
words: But just in a way- isn't that a way that men kept women in the home?

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah, kept 'em pregnant.

speaker: SS
words: Taking care of the kids and them.

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah, hide their shoes and keep 'em pregnant! (Chuckles) That was

speaker: LGC
words: kind of a joke, you know. Hide their shoes and keep 'em pregnant!

speaker: SS
words: Hide their shoes so that they can't go and be in the world.

speaker: LGC
words: That's right, and keep 'em pregnant.

speaker: SS
words: try and look at it historically: do you think there was some sort of

speaker: SS
words: revolution that went on in your generation that the women got wise and

speaker: SS
words: made a?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think there was. I just know there was, because— The Depres

speaker: LGC
words: sion hit and there was no premium on big families then. Women were

speaker: LGC
words: just frantic when they found they were pregnant again. And how were

speaker: LGC
words: they going to feed 'em? How were they going to pay the doctor bill?

speaker: LGC
words: It was a frightening, terrifying experience. And I think all of them

speaker: LGC
words: just determined, "Now there's got to be a way out of this."

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it was generally the woman who decided not to have a big

speaker: SS
words: family rather than a joint decision? It was a personal decision.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. I think . women had to do it, I think so. I de

speaker: LGC
words: finitely think so.

speaker: SS
words: It wouldn't be something that the husband and wife would sit down over

speaker: SS
words: supper and just have a big discussion about?

speaker: LGC
words: No. I think not. I think not.

speaker: SS
words: Then it was really as the women'fs feminist movement would say was ta

speaker: SS
words: king control of your own body.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right. Yes, I think that's right. I'm sure of that.

speaker: SS
words: Now one more thing; the difference between a farm family again and

speaker: SS
words: these middle class town families we were talking about before: Would

speaker: SS
words: these middle class ladies in Moscow in the teens and twenties, were they

speaker: SS
words: more likely to have smaller families than the country people?

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. They seemed to have a smaller family^,

words: Do you think that was their own way of these women to trying to carve

speaker: SS
words: out more independence for themselves and to have more freedom?

speaker: LGC
words: I think so, I think maybe. And they had intended all along to have

speaker: LGC
words: fewer children. And I don't know that they were more intelligent.

speaker: LGC
words: Maybe they had access to more knowledge. They got out among 'em, they

speaker: LGC
words: talked together. And over the years there were certain doctors who

speaker: LGC
words: would perform abortions, you know. And they were knowledgeable of

speaker: LGC
words: where to go to find these doctors. But rural women didn't have

speaker: LGC
words: access to that kind of information. And I think that that's right.

speaker: SS
words: And this would probably be true of Moscow as compared to a small town

speaker: SS
words: like Troy or Genesee or Kendrick, would be more like the country?

speaker: SS
words: Right?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Bigger families.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right.

speaker: SS
words: But ink city like Moscow, they were more exposed to these newer cur

speaker: SS
words: rents.

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. To these newer thoughts. I think that's true. I think

speaker: LGC
words: that's very true. But the Depression wasn't all bad, you see because

speaker: LGC
words: out of it grew some forward looking things. Hot lunch program for the

speaker: LGC
words: children. Milk for every child. Free innoculations. Smaller families

speaker: LGC
words: Planned parenthood. All those things grew out of the great necessity

speaker: LGC
words: and the dire need of those depression years.

speaker: SS
words: Do you feel that the Depression directly made a lot of people decide

speaker: SS
words: not to have big families?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes, I think so. They couldn't support the ones they had

speaker: SS
words: Do you feel that the Depression directly made a-

speaker: LGC
words: A lot of changes. Oh, yes, I think so, definitely. They couldn't sup

speaker: LGC
words: port the ones they had, you know. And I think it just drove them to

speaker: LGC
words: see the great necessity of doing these things. I'm sure that's right.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was much of a realization- growing realization that

speaker: SS
words: as women- as people ought to have more of a life of their own indepen

speaker: SS
words: dent of just families.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. I think with the reading. With the cultural revolu

speaker: LGC
words: tion more girls went to college, for instance and they come back with

speaker: LGC
words: other thoughts. More girls right along were going to college all the

speaker: LGC
words: time. And studied sociology and you heard about limiting families and

speaker: LGC
words: you heard about taking care of the ones you had, about the psycholo

speaker: LGC
words: gical care and about self-fulfillment and all that sort of thing. And

speaker: LGC
words: I think those all had their effect. I'm sure they did.

speaker: SS
words: When we talked about affection in families

speaker: SS
words: Was the dispenser of affection in the family?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I do. I think it was the mother.

speaker: SS
words: In country and town both?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, I do, I think so. I think the mother was the one they went to to

speaker: LGC
words: talk to and.comfort when they cut their finger and stubbed their toe.

speaker: LGC
words: I think it was the mothers they went to. It was the mothers that put

speaker: LGC
words: them to bed and dressed them; gave them their baths and took care of

speaker: LGC
words: them when they were sick. I think so.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the father in those days was a remote figure for his

speaker: SS
words: children?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, more so than now, I think. There wasn't that camaraderie near

speaker: LGC
words: so much. He was more the ruler of the house. He was the man you plea

speaker: LGC
words: sed. He was the breadwinner. I think so.

speaker: SS
words: I have read that- people saying that fathers tended to be closer to

speaker: SS
words: their daughters than to their sons. That fathers were really kind ofyou

speaker: SS
words: know, prized- not that they didn't prize their sons, but there was

speaker: SS
words: a closer relationship between father and daughter that encouraged the

speaker: SS
words: daughters when they grew up to be more independent. Now I wonder if

speaker: SS
words: that's true. I don't know if it was coming from the father or whether

speaker: SS
words: it was really coming from the mother.

speaker: LGC
words: I don't think I can answer that. I just don't know. I didn't know

speaker: LGC
words: enough people well enough. In our family I think my father had great

speaker: LGC
words: pride in the sons. You were supposed to have sons to carry on the name.

speaker: LGC
words: That's the old country idea. The sons inherited. It was for sons.

speaker: LGC
words: And that of course, is an old country concept about - the sons will

speaker: LGC
words: carry on the name. So many sons and don't mention the daughters, you

speaker: LGC
words: know. And many of the people I knew lived around us were from the

speaker: LGC
words: old countries. And I think most of them had more of that feeling.

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I think this is being presented as more of a city- as a town kind

speaker: SS
words: of thing.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. Where the fathers babied the daughters. The daughters were sweet.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. I think maybe in the cities that might be, but in the country

speaker: LGC
words: the boys- the boys were an advantage, they were so many hired helpthey

speaker: LGC
words: were going to have help on the farm to do the work. The girls

speaker: LGC
words: would not be much help. They would marry some other man, but.these

speaker: LGC
words: boys were to come up. We have to get more land for them. We've got

speaker: LGC
words: to buy that other 160. I have so many boys coming up. There was lots

speaker: LGC
words: of that went on. Lots of talk about, we must acquire land for the sake

speaker: LGC
words: of our sons, you know. Lots of that

words: Yet, when you look at these large families and the very limited amount

speaker: SS
words: of land, there must have been an awful lot of kids had to leave when

speaker: SS
words: they grew up, because they couldn't be supported.

speaker: LGC
words: They couldn't be supported. didn't want to stay. The farm life

speaker: LGC
words: had been hard on them and a lot of them wanted to go to something else.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, these boys having to work in the fields?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, the boys had to work in the fields. It had been hard work. And

speaker: LGC
words: a lot of them didn't want anymore of it.

speaker: SS
words: So there probably must have been many more of your generation left

speaker: SS
words: than stayed. Wouldn't you say?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think so. And then especially when the Depression hit and the

speaker: LGC
words: prices were so bad nobody wanted to farm. Then the war came on and

words: entered a new phase entirely, you see. So many opportunities in all

speaker: LGC
words: directions were opened up. And the kids went away to battle and lear

speaker: LGC
words: ned other things and didn't want to go back to farm life.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the First World War had that effect too?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I do. I think so, too. I think the First World War.

speaker: SS
words: So it meant that having been in the army that the boys saw other pos

speaker: SS
words: sibilities?

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. Other ways of life.

speaker: SS
words: Which

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right. You know, "How are they gonna keep them down on

speaker: LGC
words: the farm after they've seen Paree?" But there was that feeling, that

speaker: LGC
words: there was more liberty, more freedom, more adventure, more wonderful

speaker: LGC
words: things than following plows and the crops. Yes lot of 'em didn't

speaker: LGC
words: want to go back to it, although lots of 'em did.

speaker: SS
words: But then there really wasn't enough opportunities, say in Moscow in

speaker: SS
words: business for them.

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, no, no.

speaker: SS
words: They really had to get out,

speaker: LGC
words: They had to get out anyway, yes, that's right, they did. And there

speaker: LGC
words: were Hny other opportunities open.

speaker: SS
words: Did most of them go to the West Coast?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Rather than East, most of them went to-

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, like California, yes. California drew lots of these young people,

speaker: LGC
words: And the coast; Seattle, Portland and all up and down, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Or to Spokane?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. That's right,, the centers. That's right.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: between those who left and those who stayed. What would make people

speaker: SS
words: decide to leave. The people who left we can't talk to.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, that's right. The ones that stayed- that's exactly right. Yes.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder what some of the differences would be?

speaker: LGC

speaker: LGC
words: the rotation of the crops and the coming of the seasons, you know.

speaker: LGC
words: And they wouldn't have done anything else. But the bad years, the

speaker: LGC
words: Depression is really what took them off the farms, I think. They

speaker: LGC
words: thought that just over the hill was something better. Although the

speaker: LGC
words: boys that came out here, like from the CC camps from New York, my,

speaker: LGC
words: they would loved to have all stayed if they could. So that's the way

words: it goes.

words: a bit of anti-German sentiment during the war.

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes, very much. Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What have you heard about that, or seen during that time?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I saw- there were some boys that got draft deferments for this

speaker: LGC
words: and that other reason, and they rode 'em on a rail and they took off

speaker: LGC
words: their clothes and tarred and feathered some of them. Some of them as

speaker: LGC
words: old men dying still resented and remembered those violent episodes.

speaker: LGC
words: I remember when they mashed out store windows at Uniontownbhat said

speaker: LGC
words: Kraut on it. And Kraut on the window. Nobody would eat kraut. Throw

speaker: LGC
words: you know, the Kraut out, they were Germans. And all that was pretty. I remember

speaker: LGC
words: the Williamson store, he went in and gathered up every

speaker: LGC
words: thing that was made in Germany, and had a big bonfire in the mid

speaker: LGC
words: dle of the street, you know. Although he had many good German friends

speaker: LGC
words: all over the county that had helped make him rich. And there was all

speaker: LGC
words: that went on, you know. And some people changed their name. And if

speaker: LGC
words: it was a German name- we'll just change our name. We don't want any

speaker: LGC
words: thing to do with it. And there was lots of that, just that kind of

speaker: LGC
words: hysteria going on .

speaker: SS
words: This deferment business- this was German boys who didn't have to go

speaker: SS
words: into the army?

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. Some of them said their fathers were sick and dying,

speaker: LGC
words: and their father had so much land they had to stay home and farm it

speaker: LGC
words: for them and they got they called them farm deferments. And a lot of

speaker: LGC
words: those men felt badly later because they didn't share in the great ad

speaker: LGC
words: venture that the other boys had had. And there was great resentment

speaker: LGC
words: against them. A lot of them stayed home and married the belle of the

speaker: LGC
words: town, you know and didn't have to go to war and all the other kids re

speaker: LGC
words: sented that and held it against them even after they got to be old men

speaker: LGC
words: they still remembered, you hadn't gone and you chickened out.

speaker: SS
words: But this actual beating up of these fellows? Did that actually happen?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, yes. That happened here, too, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Would it be mostly in the country or Moscow?

speaker: LGC
words: I think the Saturday night businesses, you know and like at the dances

speaker: LGC
words: they'd get a few drinks and then they were looking for fights, you know.

speaker: LGC
words: hearing

speaker: SS
words: Can you actually remember of anyone tarred and feathered here?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. I know of one case where they tarred and feathered and rode him in life

speaker: LGC
words: on the rail.. And he said if he had it to do over again, he would have

speaker: LGC
words: gone, he would have been glad to have gone but his father- he was the

speaker: LGC
words: only boy among a big bunch of girls, and the father and mother both

speaker: LGC
words: cried so and took on so, that he asked for a farm deferment although

speaker: LGC
words: he was able.

speaker: SS
words: Did that happen in Moscow or Genesee?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, the folks lived about half way in between- out. And the Moscow

speaker: LGC
words: board is what gave him the deferment.. But the man was sorry all his

speaker: LGC
words: life for it.

speaker: SS
words: But he was German?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. He was of German descent.

speaker: SS
words: It is my impression of it that German people were expected to be superpatriotic,

speaker: SS
words: more so than anyone else.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, some of them had to bend over backwards. In fact, when they sold

words: war bonds they'd go out to these German farmers, you know, and just put

words: them right on the spot. They'd made fortunes and "We expect you to

words: buy so many thousand dollars worth of war bonds." And they did. Lots

speaker: LGC
words: of them went all out, as you say, to prove their patriotism. They

speaker: LGC
words: didn't want anything to do with this war. But a lot of them werethey'd

speaker: LGC
words: been brought up, this was their home country, this was just a

speaker: LGC
words: temporary thing, but their home and their loyalty were back in Germany.

speaker: LGC
words: And there was some of that alright. But they were among the older people.

words: Germany some day?

speaker: LGC
words: I think maybe they had planned maybe to go back some day. But they

words: had closer ties. They were older people. The old folks that had come

words: maybe half grown from Germany. And they always thought, "We'll make

words: a fortune in America and go back to the homeland." And that was hard

words: on them.

speaker: SS
words: Well, this young man who was tarred and feathered- how could he have

speaker: SS
words: ever like those people that had done that? I mean, if it was me, I

speaker: SS
words: think I would have always-

speaker: LGC
words: Resented it. Well, didn't..

speaker: SS
words: Were these people that he knew?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, oh, yes. Boys he'd grown up with. Yes. Yes. Of course there'd

speaker: LGC
words: been some drinking too you know. And in the heat of it all, you know

words: how those things go. He didn't live to be a very old man, so it was

words: all answered for him then. It was all answered, but I do know that

speaker: LGC

speaker: SS
words: It really doesn't matter.

speaker: LGC
words: I don't think it matters. I don't really remember. But anyway they

words: roughed him up an awful lot and it was bad and they did put the tar

words: and feathers on and they rode him on a rail.

speaker: SS
words: That's the classic-

speaker: LGC
words: Yeah, example.

speaker: SS
words: Klan.

speaker: LGC
