words: out burning crosses even and going around in sheets and pillow slips.

speaker: LGC
words: And it never lasted very long. And of course, it was against the Catholies

speaker: LGC
words: and not against the Negroes.. The Ku Klux Klan was an anti-Ca

speaker: LGC
words: tholic group. And I think that the people who belonged were not very

speaker: LGC
words: high grade intelligence. Not very^intelligent. And they did have a

speaker: LGC
words: Ku Klux Klan going around here for a while.

speaker: SS
words: This anti-Catholic feeling- would they burn crosses at people's homes

speaker: SS
words: who were Catholic? Would that be the type of thing they would do?

speaker: LGC
words: I think they were going up on Moscow Mountain to burn them as I re

speaker: LGC
words: member it. Go up on Moscow Mountain so you could see it. And some of

speaker: LGC
words: said they wouldn't let 'em go because they'd set the trees on fire.

speaker: LGC
words: But they were out marching around in white sheets and pillow slips.

speaker: LGC
words: Crazy!

speaker: SS
words: What time was it in years? I've heard it was the early ;20's. Does

speaker: SS
words: that sound right to you?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I'd say it was the early '20's. Yes, the early '20's. '22.

speaker: SS
words: How did people react to them? Do you think that some were scared by

speaker: SS
words: them?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I certainly wasn't. I took a look at the kind of people that

speaker: LGC
words: belonged and I thought, "Just how stupid can you get?"

speaker: SS
words: It was more or less known who belonged?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, it was more or less known, sure, and they hand out these little

speaker: LGC
words: anti-Catholic books and they were passing them around and just a lot

speaker: LGC
words: of hocus-pocus, you know. Just ridiculous. And all you had to do

speaker: LGC
words: was take a look at the kind of people that belonged to it and you knew

speaker: LGC
words: it wasn't very important.

speaker: SS
words: Were many of the people from the South who belonged?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, I don't know. All the ones I knew—

speaker: SS
words: Because the Klan did start in the South and I just wondered whether

speaker: SS
words: it was the same organization here.

speaker: LGC
words: It wasn't against the colored people at all, it was just against the

speaker: LGC
words: Catholics. But there were magazines published even, that came from

speaker: LGC
words: some headquarters in the East, anti-Catholic things. I remember once

speaker: LGC
words: they had some kind of a nun- escaped from a convent and she was going

speaker: LGC
words: to tell all. And one of the churches sponsored her and of all disgus

speaker: LGC
words: ting, revolting things. You could tell the woman was mentally deran

speaker: LGC
words: ged. She just went on and on about the goings on. And there were a

speaker: LGC
words: lot of men standing around there guarding it and someone said to me,

speaker: LGC
words: "oh, those are Ku Klux Klan members. They've come here to protect her

speaker: LGC
words: so she can get up and talk." I certainly had no part in that and

speaker: LGC
words: didn't want any part. I did happen to go with some women to hear this

speaker: LGC
words: woman that night. I took some old lady that wanted to go hear all

speaker: LGC
words: about it, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Sensational.

speaker: LGC
words: Sensational. Heavens, just disgusting. She hardly opened her mouth

speaker: LGC
words: when I thought, "She's a psycopath of some kind."

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the Catholics were relatively separate from the rest

speaker: SS
words: of the town in Moscow in those days?

speaker: LGC
words: No, I don't think they could have driven a wedge in any way. Although

speaker: LGC
words: I was brought up in this family where we had such great respect for

speaker: LGC
words: religious tolerance. And my father had come from just out of Belfast And he'd heard

speaker: LGC
words: that all his life,

speaker: LGC
words: the trouble and he said it was just nothing but a source of trouble.

speaker: LGC
words: We all worship the same God and some of our neighbors were Catholics

speaker: LGC
words: and very good friends, the first to come and help you, you know. And

speaker: LGC
words: we were so very friendly. Good Catholic neighbors.

speaker: SS
words: In Moscow you were.

speaker: LGC
words: In Moscow I was still just as friendly with the little girls that

speaker: LGC
words: came down from the convent. I thought they had better manners and

speaker: LGC
words: were better trained. And I never have had any feeling at all myself.

speaker: LGC
words: But I think maybe there were some, I think there were some ths.t- "Oh,

speaker: LGC
words: my, they were Catholics." And even in high school, the kids that came

speaker: LGC
words: over from the convent high school to the Moscow Public School, because

speaker: LGC
words: it was broader and more kids there. Some people had a- some of the

speaker: LGC
words: other girls in the school would say, "Well, if you're real stupid, you

speaker: LGC
words: have to go to the convent, but the smarter ones go to the Moscow High

speaker: LGC
words: School." And it was so unkind. It had nothing to do with it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know why they didn't like Catholics then, or what the reasons

speaker: SS
words: they gave?

speaker: LGC
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: Was it the Pope that they objected to?

speaker: LGC
words: I really don't know.

speaker: SS
words: One more controversial group that the Klan reminds me of, and that is

speaker: SS
words: not a group that had anything in common with them was the IWWs.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And what I want to ask you about; in the lumber country the IWWs had

speaker: SS
words: a pretty good reputation among a great many people having improved

speaker: SS
words: the conditions. But the view from Moscow was quite the opposite.

speaker: LCG
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: I understand. They were really disliked up there very much.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, They had no part with it. My husband's people here ran a thre

speaker: LGC
words: shing machine. The Snows where I lived and taught school for four

speaker: LGC
words: years, they ran a threshing machine and they had to be awfully careful

speaker: LGC
words: who they hired. They had IWWs and they called them Wobblies, and they

speaker: LGC
words: would throw a bomb into the machine, into the workings and blow up

speaker: LGC
words: the machines that summer. That was '22, I think, the summer.

speaker: SS
words: Was that real as a danger or was it something-

speaker: LGC
words: They did it a couple of times, yes they did it here on the Clyde farm.

speaker: SS
words: The IWWs did?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. They thre^one in and it went off with an explosion. Didn't blow

speaker: LGC
words: the whole thing to pieces, but it did explode in the machine.

speaker: SS
words: They were sure that it was IWWs that did it?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And not just a smut fire?

speaker: LGC
words: No, they said that this was IWWs. One of the boys got sick here and

speaker: LGC
words: he was a card carrying IWW, and the men took him to the hospital and

speaker: LGC
words: so on and took care of him, but he carried one of those blackjacks,

speaker: LGC
words: with the brass ball on the end, he carried that an IWW card and

speaker: LGC
words: they were to notify IWWs. And there were a couple of others that were.

speaker: LGC
words: And of course, they strove for shorter hours, and you know, the far

speaker: LGC
words: mers were noted for getting up at daylight and working by the lantern

speaker: LGC
words: til midnight. And they fell in disrepute in these communities because

speaker: LGC
words: of the violent way. Had it just been, "We want shorter hours." And

speaker: LGC
words: that sort of thing. Better food. And they had such wonderful food.

speaker: LGC
words: All these women were super cooks and they.each one outdid the other

speaker: LGC
words: trying to get the nicest meals in the world for them. So they couldn't

speaker: LGC
words: complain about the food.

speaker: SS
words: I think it was more in the lumber camps they were complaining about

speaker: SS
words: the food.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. But they did complain about the long hours. And of course, har

speaker: LGC
words: vest is the time when they have to work long hours to save the crop.

speaker: LGC
words: So there was certainly two sides. The farmers were very resentful of

speaker: LGC
words: anybody that wasn't willing to go out and put everything into it for

speaker: LGC
words: the short harvest season.

speaker: SS
words: They didn't feel that the IWWs were shirkers when they worked, or did

speaker: SS
words: they?

speaker: LGC
words: No. They just wanted to quit too early. They wanted to quit too

speaker: LGC
words: early and not begin - they wanted shorter days.

speaker: SS
words: So they found it difficult to hire without hiring IWWs? I mean, there

speaker: SS
words: were so many of the hands- the traveling people were.

speaker: LGC
words: That's right. Lots of the traveling people that came through were.

speaker: LGC
words: And there was a feeling on the part of the farmers of resentment. But

speaker: LGC
words: I think once again, that was a good thing. Because it got new ways -

speaker: LGC
words: combines and tractors where you didn't need these great crews of men.

speaker: LGC
words: It got so one man could farm half the country and they didn't need all

speaker: LGC
words: this great army of men to work for them.

speaker: SS
words: But you feel sure from what the Clydes said that they had no doubt that

speaker: SS
words: it was actually set by the IWWs?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. They thought that this one explosion was set by the IWWs.

speaker: SS
words: Did they know who did it?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, I think they thought this man that later got sick and they had to

speaker: LGC
words: card

speaker: LGC
words: take him that was the carrying man, they that he was kind of

speaker: LGC
words: a leader.

speaker: SS
words: They actually found—

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, they found his card in his pocket.

speaker: SS
words: But I mean in the machine- they found the scrap.

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, there was evidence of something being thrown in. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: And this was about '22?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, '22, around in there. After the First World War, probably '22, '23.

speaker: SS
words: The attitude towards these migratory workers that came through: What

speaker: SS
words: did the farmers really think?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, they were all- the farmers were just hard working people them

speaker: LGC
words: selves. And in those days the formula was for everybody- if you

speaker: LGC
words: worked hard, you mafce a lot of money and you could have all these things

speaker: LGC
words: see. There was never any idea of asking for government subsidies and

speaker: LGC
words: that sort of thing. You got out and you earned it yourself. If you

speaker: LGC
words: wanted your children to be educated and have things, there

speaker: LGC
words: was nothing to do but you worked like that. Get up early and work late.

speaker: LGC
words: And they couldn't see the other person's side of it. They were willing

speaker: LGC
words: to do it, because it was their bread and butter on the line; their

speaker: LGC
words: farms on the line. But they expected the men to do it and that's where

speaker: LGC
words: the trouble came. I cooked for the Snow boys when one noon all the men

speaker: LGC
words: lined up outside and after dinner, they ate a nice dinner first, then

speaker: LGC
words: they all lined up and they weren't going to go back to the field, they

speaker: LGC
words: were on strike. And it took quite a lot of negotiating out there to

speaker: LGC
words: get 'em back. And it was getting late in the harvest season and there

speaker: LGC
words: was just nothing to do but meet whatever demand there was. And it was

speaker: LGC
words: met and they negotiated and got it done.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how much they negotiated for?

speaker: LGC
words: Oh, probably wasn't over fifty cents or a dollar a day. So it wasn't

speaker: LGC
words: so much as all that. But anyway it was all settled.

speaker: SS
words: Was one man their spokesman?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, they had one man that talked for them.

speaker: SS
words: Can't imagine it created goodwill between them.

speaker: LGC
words: No, and when it didn't really form an adventure when all the neigh

speaker: LGC
words: bors came in and did each others crops- "We'll do this one first. And

speaker: LGC
words: here's old Mr. so-and-so, he needs the money, we'll go to him next.

speaker: LGC
words: But when they started having to go out and hire outsiders; strangers

speaker: LGC
words: that came into the country, that's what took the fun and the joy out

speaker: LGC
words: of it.

speaker: SS
words: Why did they have to go to outsiders? What happened that they couldn't

speaker: SS
words: use just local people anymore?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I imagine that over the years the kids had grown up and gone to

speaker: LGC
words: that First World War. Lot of them didn't come back to the farms. There

speaker: LGC
words: weren't big families. And then as the farmers got to farming, they

speaker: LGC
words: had other ways of combining- of threshing. The Clydes had their th

speaker: LGC
words: reshing right here and Snows next door they had their threshing machine.

speaker: LGC
words: Each one had their own.

speaker: SS
words: Had their own threshing machine?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. Had their own threshing machines. And so many people with five

speaker: LGC
words: or six hundred acres- each one had his own threshing machine, instead

speaker: LGC
words: of all going together and hav ing twenty-two headerboxes and everybody

speaker: LGC
words: doing this one and that. Each one was doing his own thing.

speaker: SS
words: Then they had to get outside help.

speaker: LGC
words: Then they had to get outside help.

speaker: SS
words: Were they still steam threshers at that time?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes. They were steam threshers.

speaker: SS
words: And they had the long belt between?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, they had the belt.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why they decided to get their own instead of- Didn't it work

speaker: SS
words: pretty well?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, it work when they went together, it did. Well, I think they

speaker: LGC
words: had such long- they would start early like down toward the rim and come

speaker: LGC
words: on up. As each farm expanded and got more land then they needed thre

speaker: LGC
words: shing machines. Instead of this man and that man each owning 160, here

speaker: LGC
words: would be one man with 500 acres and the next one with 5 and the next

speaker: LGC
words: one with five and if you had 500 acres, you almost had to go get your

speaker: LGC
words: own threshing machine to get it cut. I think that was probably the

speaker: LGC
words: way of it. But that's the way I remember the IWW deal. I just re

speaker: LGC
words: member that one summer.

speaker: SS
words: That year of the strike is that the same summer?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, it was the same summer, about '22 and '23, those were the years.

speaker: LGC
words: And these men, a lot of them had come from Colfax. Colfax is a little

speaker: LGC
words: earlier harvest field than here and they'd gotten men that had come

speaker: LGC
words: there and these men had hired them from all up in town; transients that

speaker: LGC
words: had come in looking for harvest jobs and brought them out. And the

speaker: LGC
words: men on the Snow machine knew the men here on the Clyde machine- they

speaker: LGC
words: had known each other before.

speaker: SS
words: Did they both strike?

speaker: LGC
words: Well, I don't think these folks struck, they just tried to blow up the

speaker: LGC
words: machine that day. And then one of them got so sick and in this process

speaker: LGC
words: of identifying him and trying to get word to his relatives and so on,

speaker: LGC
words: they had to go through his pockets, and they found he was an IWW and

speaker: LGC
words: that he had this blackjack andjie had come equippd for trouble.

speaker: LGC
words: 8S: Where did most of these fellows come from? These itinerant guys that

speaker: LGC
words: were working in the harvest? Did you know where they were from?

speaker: LGC
words: I really dont' know. I don't know where. I never did know where they

speaker: LGC
words: came from. There were very few local people- there were two or three

speaker: LGC
words: local men from town on the crew, but once again, they knew the Clydes,

speaker: LGC
words: they knew the Snows and they went right on with the work and they tried

speaker: LGC
words: to hold the thing together because they were personal friends, and men

speaker: LGC
words: who knew them. And men that were going to have to go on living in the

speaker: LGC
words: community, too. But these others were transients who had come in and

speaker: LGC
words: were just waiting for harvest jobs to open. They had put in time over

speaker: LGC
words: at Colfax and wandered over here just a little later.

speaker: SS
words: After, what happened? Did they have to still keep going to out

speaker: SS
words: siders to thresh?

speaker: LGC
words: Yes, quite a while. There was quite a little of that around. But then

speaker: LGC
words: they started in going into combines. About '24 and '25 everybody was

speaker: LGC
words: buying combines, and didn't need so many men, see. Even back in '22

speaker: LGC
words: Snows had had a horse drawn combine. They got rid of it. But then

speaker: LGC
words: they got more horse drawn combines and then pretty soon there were tra

speaker: LGC
words: ctor drawn combines. And then pretty soon they were selfpropelled.

speaker: LGC
words: But all this trouble with the IWWs helped to hurry up the getting of the

speaker: LGC
words: combines into the field. It hurried it. They figured it was the only

speaker: LGC
words: way to relieve the labor problem was to get combines that they could

speaker: LGC
words: run them in their own family. So I think that's the way that worked.

speaker: LGC
words: And then of course, in '29 came the Depression, and that was a new ball

speaker: LGC
words: of wax.
